does: This paper introduces UniEditBench, a unified evaluation framework for both image and video editing models that operates across different editing paradigms—reconstruction-based and instruction-driven methods. The benchmark defines a structured taxonomy of nine image operations (Add, Remove, Replace, Change, Stroke-based, Extract, Adjust, Count, Reorder) and eight video operations, including challenging compositional tasks like counting and spatial reordering. To address the high computational cost of using large multimodal models as judges, the authors distill a 235B parameter MLLM (Qwen3-VL) into lightweight 4B and 8B evaluators that provide multi-dimensional scoring across structural fidelity, text alignment, and other quality dimensions. This enables scalable, cost-effective automatic evaluation while maintaining alignment with human preferences.
connects: This work builds on a lineage of visual editing evaluation research that has historically remained fragmented across image-only and video-only benchmarks with domain-specific metrics. It responds to limitations in prior evaluation approaches where automatic metrics often misalign with human judgment, and addresses the emerging problem of evaluating instruction-based editing methods alongside traditional reconstruction tasks. The paper extends recent advances in multimodal large language models as evaluators—a paradigm shift from hand-crafted metrics—while acknowledging that deploying full-scale MLLMs is computationally prohibitive. The distillation approach builds on knowledge distillation literature, adapting it to create efficient evaluator models that maintain the judgment quality of much larger foundation models.
enables: UniEditBench establishes a unified evaluation standard that future image and video editing methods can use for fair cross-paradigm comparison, removing barriers to systematic benchmarking progress in the field. By providing a structured taxonomy and efficient evaluation infrastructure, it enables more rapid iteration on editing architectures and enables researchers without access to large computational resources to rigorously evaluate their systems. The distilled MLLM evaluators open pathways for developing lighter-weight, specialized judges for visual understanding tasks more broadly. This work also points toward systematic evaluation of compositional editing capabilities—complex multi-step operations like counting and reordering—which remain relatively understudied in visual editing. The framework could facilitate development of more robust editing models by providing fine-grained dimensional feedback on specific failure modes.
